optionList.content: Making notes of the client's condition in the electronic health record (EHR) and filing an incident report
Noting the client's condition in the medication administration record (MAR) and filing an incident report
Documenting the client's condition in the intake and output (l&O) form and filing an incident report
Writing notes of the client's condition in the discharge summary form and filing an incident report
Making notes of the client's condition in the electronic health record (EHR) and filing an incident report
Noting the client's condition in the medication administration record (MAR) and filing an incident report
Documenting the client's condition in the intake and output (l&O) form and filing an incident report
Writing notes of the client's condition in the discharge summary form and filing an incident report

studentQuestionArray.content: When collecting a suctioned specimen, the nurse applies pressure by placing the finger over the suction control port for more than 10 seconds. The nurse notices that the client starts sweating profusely and coughing. Which steps taken by the nurse after caring for the client is correct?
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationales

Option 1:The nurse should document the problems faced by the client, the interdisciplinary plan of care (IPOC), and the clinical findings in the EHR. The nurse should file an incident report of this untoward incident to alert hospital administrators, which will help prevent such incidents from occurring.
Option 2:An MAR is used to document information about the drug, including contraindications, if any. The client's condition should not be documented in an MAR. Any untoward incident should be filed in an incident report to alert hospital administrators.
Option 3:An I&O form is used to document the fluid balance of a client. An incident report should be completed for any unusual occurrence. This incident does not qualify for documentation on an I&O form.
Option 4:An untoward incident that has been filed in the incident report is not mentioned in the discharge summary form.

studentQuestionArray.content: A nurse administers liquid medication to a toddler too quickly, which causes the toddler to choke. Where should the nurse document the issue to alert facility administrators after taking care of the client?
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationales

Option 1:MARs display a record of medications administered to the client. This does not alert hospital administrators.
Option 2:An EHR is an electronic record display of medications and interventions that have been planned for and given to the client. This is not used to alert hospital administrators.
Option 3:The nurse should document the incident in the incident report to prevent such occurrences from happening in the future. This helps alert hospital administrators.
Option 4:The narrative chart contains the chronology of progress that the client has shown as a result of medications administered in the hospital. This is not used to alert hospital administrators.

optionList.content: Document the incident in the medication administration record (MAR).
Describe the incident in the discharge summary.
Document the incident in the medication administration record (MAR).
Describe the incident in the discharge summary.

studentQuestionArray.content: A nurse unintentionally places his or her hands into the mouth of a client during a seizure and is bitten severely. Which appropriate nursing action should a nurse take to notify hospital administrators?
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationales

Option 1:The nurse should file an incident report to alert hospital administrators. This will help create a safer place for the staff to work and prevent such incidences.
Option 2:The nurse can notify the nurse-in-charge, who can notify the primary health-care provider, if required. However, this will not alert hospital administrators.
Option 3:The nurse should not document this incident in the MAR as it only contains information about medications prescribed to the client.
Option 4:A discharge summary contains data about the client's condition at the time of discharge and medications to be continued. The nurse should not document the untoward incident in this summary.

optionList.content: Contact the hospital chief executive officer (CEO).
Report the matter in written form to the health-care provider.
Contact the hospital chief executive officer (CEO).
Report the matter in written form to the health-care provider.

studentQuestionArray.content: A nurse is unable to administer intravenous (IV) medications to a client on time due to the unavailability of the appropriate IV equipment. After ensuring client safety, who should the nurse inform?
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationales

Option 1:The nurse should follow the chain of command and report the issue to the nurse manager.
Option 2:The housekeeping department is not responsible for keeping track of client care supplies. The nurse need not notify this department.
Option 3:If essential client care supplies are unavailable, the nurse should notify the nursing manager and file an incident report to alert facility administrators.
Option 4:The health-care provider needs to be notified regarding the delay in medication administration. However, an incident report should be submitted to the hospital administration, not to the provider.

studentQuestionArray.content: The nurse reports that the client has acquired an endogenous health care-related infection. What could be the most probable cause for the client's health condition?
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationales

Option 1:An infection caused by a pathogen acquired from the healthcare environment, such as a contaminated needle, is termed as an exogenous health care-related infection.
Option 2:An infection caused by a contaminated stethoscope is an example of an exogenous health care-related infection.
Option 3:Certain treatments such as chronic administration of antibiotics can cause normal body flora to rapidly multiply and cause infection. This type of infection arising from the client's own natural flora is called an endogenous health care-related infection.
Option 4:IV saline is administered either by an injection or a catheter, which are medical instruments that are a part of the health-care environment. Therefore, an infection arising due to IV saline administration is termed as an exogenous health care-related infection.

optionList.content: File the incident in the intake and output (I&O).
File the incident in the Electronic Health Record(EHR) system.
File the incident in the medication administration record (MAR).
File the incident in an incident report.
File the incident in the intake and output (I&O).
File the incident in the Electronic Health Record(EHR) system.
File the incident in the medication administration record (MAR).
File the incident in an incident report.

studentQuestionArray.content: What action should the nurse take to alert the authorities if he or she suffers a back injury while lifting a client on the hospital premises?
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationales

Option 1:The I&O form contains data about the fluid intake and output and the client's fluid balance.
Option 2:EHR is not used to document an unusual occurrence; it includes clinical findings, laboratory tests, and progress notes of the nurse, primary health-care provider, and other providers.
Option 3:The MAR is not used for recording any untoward incidents.
Option 4:The nurse must file an occurrence report in regards to the back injury. This will help the hospital authority to track the problem and to create a safer place to work.

studentQuestionArray.content: A frail client falls from the hospital bed at night while trying to go to the toilet and suffers a patellar fracture. What is the most appropriate method for the nurse to document the incident?
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationales

Option 1:Nurse's note is a document maintained to keep details about the client care and responses to the medications.
Option 2:Health record is a document containing all the details about the care provided by the health-care providers. It also includes the client responses on medications and facts about the health history.
Option 3:Transfer reports are created when a client is transferred from one unit or facility to another.
Option 4:Occurrence report is a record on any kind of unusual incidents or accidents that occur in a facility. A client fall and injury call for a nurse to write an occurrence report.

optionList.content: The nurse should file an incident report.
The nurse should inform the health-care provider about the accident.
The nurse should document the incident in the client's medication administration record (MAR).
The nurse should ask the unlicensed assistive personnel (UAP) to document the incident in the incident report so that the nurse can continue with the pericare.
The nurse should file an incident report.
The nurse should inform the health-care provider about the accident.
The nurse should document the incident in the client's medication administration record (MAR).
The nurse should ask the unlicensed assistive personnel (UAP) to document the incident in the incident report so that the nurse can continue with the pericare.

studentQuestionArray.content: A client's urine accidentally spills on the nurse during perineal area care (pericare). What action should the nurse take to alert facility administrators about the incident?
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationales

Option 1:The nurse should file an occurrence report or incident report so that facility administrators can be notified about the accident and the organization can track problematic areas and can create a safer place to work.
Option 2:Whenever there is an accident, the nurse should first inform the nurse in charge, who would assess the situation and notify the health-care provider if necessary.
Option 3:The MAR is used to document the details of the medication administered to the client.
Option 4:When an incident occurs, a nurse must document it in the incident report.

optionList.content: Wrap with one layer of loose-fitting gauze.
Wrap with one layer of loose-fitting gauze.

studentQuestionArray.content: The nurse reports a cut on the thumb. What precaution should the nurse take to best prevent the spread of infection during client contact?
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationales

Option 1:The nurse who previously had a cut on the thumb will have the risk of spreading infection due to direct physical contact during the treatment. Therefore, the nurse should cover the wound with an occlusive dressing to prevent contamination. The nurse might contact client mucosa, blood, or secretions that could transmit organisms through the cut into the nurse's blood stream.
Option 2:A resuscitation bag is used to prevent infection through mucus membranes or airflow.
Option 3:Before and after a procedure, the hands should be washed thoroughly to ensure that skin pathogens are killed. Mere hand wash with a cut injury cannot prevent contraction of an infection during client contact.
Option 4: An occlusive dressing is a better way to prevent infection.

studentQuestionArray.content: A nurse administers morphine instead of meperidine to a client without properly checking the medication administration record (MAR). After ensuring client safety, where should the nurse document the occurrence to alert facility administrators?
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationales

Option 1:The MAR is used to record the drug name and dosage administered to the client; the medication error should not be documented here.
Option 2:The incident report is used to record any type of untoward incident within the hospital. The wrong drug administration must be documented here because this will alert facility administrators and help prevent such incidents from happening again.
Option 3:An I&O record is used to record the client's fluid balance, especially for clients with kidney disease or pulmonary edema. This is not used to document the wrong drug administration.
Option 4:The electronic checklist is used to record normal and abnormal findings and the care provided to the client. This form is not used to document medication errors.

optionList.content: File the occurrence report stating why the medication was not administered.

The nurse should make notes about the client's refusal in the medication administrationrecord (MAR) and add a nurse's note explaining why the medication
was not administered.
The nurse should document the client's refusal to take the medication on the intake and output (1&O) form and mention why the medication was not given.
The nurse should document it in the Electronic Health Record(EHR) system and mention why the medication was not given.
File the occurrence report stating why the medication was not administered.

The nurse should make notes about the client's refusal in the medication administrationrecord (MAR) and add a nurse's note explaining why the medication
was not administered.
The nurse should document the client's refusal to take the medication on the intake and output (1&O) form and mention why the medication was not given.
The nurse should document it in the Electronic Health Record(EHR) system and mention why the medication was not given.

studentQuestionArray.content: How should the nurse document a client refusing to take medication?
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationales

Option 1:The occurrence report is used to document unusual occurrences and accidents.
Option 2:The nurse should document the information in the MAR even when a drug is not administered. In addition to noting the medication details, the nurse should also add a nurse's note explaining why the medication was not given. This benefits the oncoming shift nurse to assess the client properly.
Option 3:The I&O form is used to document data about the client's fluid balance.
Option 4:The EHR is used to note clinical findings, laboratory tests, and progress notes of the nurse and primary health-care provider.

studentQuestionArray.content: Which is the most common incident reported in hospitals and health-care facilities, according to the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services?
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationales

Option 1:Equipment-related accidents occur due to equipment malfunction or improper usage. It is not the most common reportable incident.
Option 2:Falls are considered the most common reportable incident in health-care facilities. A fall occurs one in every 2,000 clients.
Option 3:Fires are caused by malfunctioning or improperly grounded equipment or by careless smoking. However, fires are not a common reportable incident.
Option 4:Improperly grounded electrical equipment results in electrical hazards. However, it is not the most common reportable incident.

optionList.content: The client is showing vague symptoms for the first time.
The client is in the stage between pathogen invasion and the first appearance of symptoms.
The infection has been reduced due to immunologic defense.
The client returns to health as the remaining number of microorganisms in the body approaches zero.
The client is showing vague symptoms for the first time.
The client is in the stage between pathogen invasion and the first appearance of symptoms.
The infection has been reduced due to immunologic defense.
The client returns to health as the remaining number of microorganisms in the body approaches zero.

studentQuestionArray.content: How does the nurse interpret an incident report regarding the incubation stage of infection in a client hospitalized due to cardiopulmonary disorder?
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationales

Option 1:In the prodromal stage, the client is exposed to the vague symptoms of the infection for the first time.
Option 2:Incubation is the period between the successful pathogen invasion and the first appearance of the symptoms.
Option 3:The immune defense of the client reduces the number of pathogenic microbes, thereby reducing the infection.
Option 4:In the convalescence stage, the client returns to health as the number of microorganisms approaches zero.

optionList.content: Describe the accident in the medication administration record (MAR).
Add the accident to the problem list.
Document the accident in an incident report.
Detail the accident in the discharge summary.
Describe the accident in the medication administration record (MAR).
Add the accident to the problem list.
Document the accident in an incident report.
Detail the accident in the discharge summary.

studentQuestionArray.content: A nurse places a client in high-Fowler's position to mobilize secretions from the apical areas of the upper lobes of the lung. Suddenly, the bed falls flat due to the lever dislodging and injures the client's back. What should the nurse do to alert authorities about such malfunctioning hospital furniture, after ensuring client safety?
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationales

Option 1:The nurse should not document the untoward incident in the MAR as this record is used to document information about prescribed and administered medication.
Option 2:The problem list is used to document all the medical issues that the client needs to be treated for. Hence, the problem of sudden lever dislodgement in the bed cannot be documented here because it is not something that the client needs to be treated for.
Option 3:The nurse should report the untoward incident to hospital administrators by filing an incident report. This is done to prevent such incidents and create a safer place for client care.
Option 4:The nurse should not document the untoward occurrence in the discharge summary because this contains the client's condition on discharge and medications to be continued.

studentQuestionArray.content: A nurse notices that a client's vital signs are fluctuating after the IV line is inserted into a wrong vein. In which document should the nurse report the erroneous catheter insertion, after ensuring client safety?
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationales

Option 1:An EHR is used to maintain the record of the client's health status, medications, and interventions. Hence, this will not have an error report.
Option 2:An incident report is the only document that maintains the record of erroneous medications and other untoward incidents. Hence, the report of erroneous catheter insertion should be recorded as an incident report.
Option 3:MARs are records of medications administered to a client. Hence, this document does not contain any report on errors made by the nurse.
Option 4:The plan of care is used to maintain the primary health-care provider's orders and the nursing care plan for addressing client's complications. Hence, no error report can be documented here.

optionList.content: The incident through an incident report
The incident through an incident report

studentQuestionArray.content: Which document should the nurses complete to alert hospital authorities of an enteral feeding tube erroneously connected to the intravenous (IV) lines?
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationales

Option 1:The POR contains the database, problem list, plan of care, and progress notes. It is updated on a regular basis for the client's status.
Option 2:The MAR is not used to document any unusual occurrence or accident because it contains the details of the medication prescribed by the primary health-care provider.
Option 3:The nurse must document this type of incident in the occurrence report. This is done to alert hospital authorities so that they can keep track of any accident and ensure safety.
Option 4:The EHR is used to document the medical findings, laboratory tests, and progress notes of the nurse, primary health-care provider, and other providers.

optionList.content: Document the error on the problem list.
Inform other nurses on the unit of the occurrence.
Report the matter to the facility's safety officer.
Document the error on the problem list.
Inform other nurses on the unit of the occurrence.
Report the matter to the facility's safety officer.

studentQuestionArray.content: A nurse inserts a urinary catheter made of latex without checking the client's allergy band. The client develops an anaphylactic reaction. What should the nurse do after taking necessary measures to ensure the client's safety?
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationales

Option 1:The nurse prepares a problem list based on a client's health records to formulate a plan of care. The nurse should not document the error in this list.
Option 2:If a client's life is endangered due to an untoward incident, the nurse should submit an incident report to facility administrators.
Option 3: The nurse should submit an incident report to facility administrators.
Option 4:The health-care facility's safety officer should be informed about pandemic disease outbreaks. The nurse should not report untoward incidents to the safety officer.

studentQuestionArray.content: The nurse reports signs of inflammation in a client after an injury. Which symptoms related to the client’s wound will the nurse report? Select all that apply.
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationales

Option 1:Inflammation is a process that is triggered by the release of histamines and other chemical mediators after an injury. Due to these changes the area around the wound becomes red and erythematous.
Option 2:Inflammation causes vasodilation and increased vascular permeability. This results in accumulation of edematous fluid in the tissue spaces around the wound.
Option 3:Intact skin is not a sign of inflammation. In fact, skin that is intact serves as a barrier and prevents entry of pathogenic microorganisms.
Option 4:The genitourinary tract is lined by mucus-secreting epithelial cells. These cells secrete mucus that helps eliminate microorganisms through urine and feces.
Option 5:The respiratory passages are lined with cilia which are tiny hair-like cells. These cells help in eliminating microorganisms from the lower airways passages and push them out of the body via the external nares. Cilia are a primary defense mechanism of the body.

studentQuestionArray.content: A nurse is injured while trying to pick up a needle that fell on the ground. What immediate actions should be taken by the nurse? Select all that apply.
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationales

Option 1:The health-care provider should be informed only if the injury is severe.
Option 2:Administering first aid to the injury will help alleviate the consequential pain caused by the injury.
Option 3:Safety personnel must be notified only when sharps need to be cleared from a particular area.
Option 4:Filing an occurrence report will get facility administrators to look into procedures that will help make the hospital environment safer for both clients and staff.
Option 5:Laboratory tests must be done if the injury bleeds. This ensures the nurse is free from infections.

optionList.content: In the intake and output (l&O) sheet
In the intake and output (l&O) sheet

studentQuestionArray.content: A nurse misreads q.o.d. for q.d. and administers the medication on a daily basis to the client, instead of giving it on alternate days. Where should the nurse document the medication error to alert hospital administrators and help the health-care provider assess the client appropriately to prevent any complications? Select all that apply.
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationales

Option 1:The discharge summary contains a report of the client's condition on discharge and is prepared when a client is transferred within the same organization, transferred to a different facility, or discharged to home. This is not used to report an untoward incident.
Option 2:The incident report contains the formal record of an unusual occurrence. This report alerts hospital administrators to track problems and analyze areas for quality improvement to create a safer place for clients.
Option 3:The EHR contains the client's vital signs and the progress notes entered by the nurse. Documenting the administered drug dose in this record will help the health-care provider assess the client appropriately.
Option 4:An I&O form contains the details of the client's fluid balance. After documenting the details in an I&O form, it is saved in the client's EHR. Any untoward incident should not be documented on this form.
Option 5:The MAR contains the details of the medication prescribed by the health-care provider and the drug administered along with the dosage and time. Documenting the administered drug dose in this record will assist the primary health-care provider to assess and prescribe new medication to the client appropriately.
